_description: Offline checks that run without internet access. Based on industry best practices, CIS benchmarks, and OWASP guidance.

21.name: FTP — Unencrypted File Transfer
21.description: FTP transmits files AND passwords in plain text. Anyone on the same network can intercept them with a simple packet capture tool.
21.recommendation: Replace FTP with SFTP (runs over SSH on port 22) or FTPS. If FTP is absolutely required, ensure it's behind a VPN.

23.name: Telnet — Completely Unencrypted Remote Access
23.description: Telnet sends EVERYTHING — usernames, passwords, commands — in plain readable text. This was acceptable in the 1970s. It is not acceptable today. Any attacker on the network can read every keystroke.
23.recommendation: DISABLE TELNET IMMEDIATELY. Replace with SSH (port 22). There is no scenario in which Telnet is acceptable on a modern network.

135.name: Windows RPC — Remote Procedure Call
135.description: Windows RPC has been the source of numerous critical vulnerabilities including the famous MS03-026 (Blaster worm) and MS08-067. When exposed to the internet it is a prime attack target.
135.recommendation: Block port 135 at the firewall from all external access. Internal access should also be reviewed.

139.name: NetBIOS — Windows Legacy File Sharing
139.description: NetBIOS is a legacy Windows protocol used for file and printer sharing. It leaks information about the network (computer names, domain info) and has been used in numerous attacks.
139.recommendation: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP if not required. Block port 139 at the firewall.

445.name: SMB — Windows File Sharing (EternalBlue Target)
445.description: SMB is the protocol used by WannaCry ransomware (2017, $4 billion damage) and NotPetya (2017, $10 billion damage). The vulnerability MS17-010 (EternalBlue) specifically targets port 445. Even patched systems should not expose SMB to the internet.
445.recommendation: NEVER expose SMB to the internet. Ensure MS17-010 patch is applied. Consider blocking SMB entirely from untrusted networks.

3389.name: RDP — Remote Desktop Protocol
3389.description: RDP is heavily targeted by ransomware operators. BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) allowed unauthenticated remote code execution. RDP exposed to the internet receives thousands of brute-force attempts per day.
3389.recommendation: Never expose RDP directly to the internet. Place behind VPN. Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA). Use a jump host / bastion server.

5900.name: VNC — Virtual Network Computing
5900.description: VNC provides graphical desktop access. When exposed to the internet it is frequently targeted by brute-force attacks. Many deployments use weak or default passwords.
5900.recommendation: Place VNC behind a VPN. Use strong, unique passwords. Consider replacing with a more modern remote access solution.

6379.name: Redis — In-Memory Database (Often Unauthenticated)
6379.description: Redis was designed for trusted internal networks and historically had no authentication by default. Thousands of exposed Redis instances have been completely compromised, with attackers reading all data, writing malicious cron jobs, or using them for cryptomining.
6379.recommendation: Redis must NEVER be internet-facing. Enable requirepass authentication. Bind to 127.0.0.1 only. Use a firewall to block external access.

27017.name: MongoDB — Database (Historically No Auth by Default)
27017.description: Early versions of MongoDB had no authentication enabled by default. Hundreds of millions of records have been exposed in MongoDB breaches. In 2017, ransomware operators wiped over 27,000 unprotected MongoDB databases.
27017.recommendation: Enable MongoDB authentication immediately. Bind to localhost. Never expose MongoDB to the internet. Use a firewall.

1433.name: Microsoft SQL Server — Database Port
1433.description: SQL Server exposed to the internet is a major attack target. Default SA (system administrator) accounts with weak passwords are frequently exploited. Remote code execution is possible via xp_cmdshell.
1433.recommendation: Database servers should NEVER be directly internet-accessible. Use a firewall. Disable SA account or use a strong password. Disable xp_cmdshell.

3306.name: MySQL — Database Port
3306.description: MySQL exposed to the internet is frequently targeted. Attackers look for weak root passwords or accounts with no password. Remote code execution is possible via SELECT INTO OUTFILE.
3306.recommendation: Bind MySQL to 127.0.0.1 only. Never expose port 3306 to the internet. Use strong unique passwords for all database users.

checks.name: SSH Version Check
checks.description: SSH versions below 7.4 have known vulnerabilities including username enumeration.

checks.name: Unencrypted HTTP Exposed
checks.description: HTTP transmits data without encryption. Credentials and session tokens can be intercepted. Should redirect to HTTPS.

checks.name: Server Version Disclosed in Banner
checks.description: The web server is advertising its software version. This helps attackers find matching vulnerabilities.

checks.name: Anonymous FTP Access
checks.description: FTP server may allow anonymous login — anyone can connect without a password.

checks.name: Telnet Service Active
checks.description: Telnet is active and transmitting data in plain text.

checks.name: SMB Port Exposed
checks.description: SMB is the target of EternalBlue/WannaCry. Even patched, this should not be internet-facing.

checks.name: RDP Exposed to Network
checks.description: Remote Desktop is a top ransomware entry point. Should be restricted to VPN access only.

Apache.recommendation: Apache 2.2 and below are end-of-life. Upgrade to Apache 2.4.x latest.

nginx.recommendation: This nginx version may be outdated. Upgrade to the latest stable release.

OpenSSH.recommendation: OpenSSH version is outdated. Upgrade to latest to patch known vulnerabilities.

vsftpd.recommendation: vsftpd 2.3.4 contained a deliberately planted backdoor (CVE-2011-2523). Remove and reinstall from trusted source immediately.

IIS.recommendation: IIS version is end-of-life. Upgrade to IIS 10 on Windows Server 2019+.

ProFTPD.recommendation: This ProFTPD version has known remote code execution vulnerabilities. Upgrade immediately.

default_credentials.description: Common default credential combinations that attackers try first
